words: SAM SCHRAGER: First, Iwas wondering about what you had heard about the importance
of the railroad, the coming of the railroad to Troy.
WILLA CUMMINGS CARLSON: Oh, well that was terribly important. When my father,
S. Charles Cummings, came out from Michigan, he came by rail as far as Colfax,
and the railroad stopped there. Now Ihave in my notes someplace how excited
the people of Moscow were waiting for this to come. But Ithink you'll find
there where Mrs. Roberts had said in her diary that Mr. Robert's family were
coming from Pennsylvania. Well Ithink they stopped by way of Illinois and
Kansas-that's the way most of that particular group of people came. They
stopped at these places because there were people who had gone ahead of them
to those places, and they stopped and visited with them awhile and then came
on. And they were so excited about them coming from Colfax and Iremember it
especially. Itook the notes down from Mrs. Roberts' diary...Getting Mr. and
Mrs. Roberts, that is, Al Roberts' father and mother, out here, and then his
brothers who come with them-and that was Frank, Cyrus and Oliver. Asnow
came. It was in the spring. Of course they weren't used to the country nor
the climate, and it turned out to be areal summer snowstorm. So before he
got to Palouse, he had to trade the bobsleds for awagon, and went on the rest
of the way with awagon. And then he had to bring them home in awagon. And
he didn't get the sleds home until the next winter I believe.

speaker: SAM
words: You mean a chinook came up?

speaker: SAM
words: UCi well of course they always say it's achinook when the snow goes off. But

speaker: SAM
words: you get asnowstorm in the last of April, and certainly in May, it was like

speaker: SAM
words: when Watts disappeared. They go off and they don't need achinook to take it

speaker: SAM
words: off (chuckles). Oust naturally the sun's up alot higher you know, and just

speaker: SAM
words: ordinary temperatures rising, and unless they rapidly fall, why they're not

speaker: SAM
words: going to keep on asnow. No snow lasts when they come that way, that is, up

speaker: SAM
words: to date. Of course there may come atime when they will, Idon't know. But

speaker: SAM
words: anyway they came home in the wagon, and Ialways thought that was interesting,

speaker: SAM
words: how they went up with the sled (chuckles)—

speaker: SAM
words: So what about the railroad coming to Troy?

speaker: WC
words: Well the railroad hadn't come to Troy yet then—so it came later, after

speaker: WC
words: the old Roberts' had come out. My father came in 1889. It was after he came

speaker: WC
words: that it went from Troy down to Kendrick, because he worked on the railroad

speaker: WC
words: line between Troy and Kendrick. But people in Moscow were terribly excited

speaker: WC
words: about the rails coming, and they began to get excited down the line. Troy

speaker: WC
words: was a tough little old town and got tougher I guess. It was a tough pioneer

speaker: WC
words: town, and then it got tougher as it got to be a railroad town. Because it

speaker: WC
words: was a railroad headquarters for quite some time, the headquarters for supplies

speaker: WC
words: for the railroad company as it built the line on down toward Kendrick see,

speaker: WC
words: down through that canyon. That was really something, driving it down there...

speaker: WC
words: Hard to find awide enough spot in the bottom of that canyon, you know, to

speaker: WC
words: set their box cars, and one thing another. Well Mrs. Roberts went down there

speaker: WC
words: and cooked one summer for the men. Idon't know that it was in the diary, I

speaker: WC
words: think it was in some little additional booklet.

speaker: SAM
words: You mentioned it in your manuscript that she did cook for the men.

speaker: UC
words: Did I? Yes, Iknow, but of course Iknow alot of that by her telling me,

speaker: UC
words: because I just ate up this stuff. I just lived along at Roberts' when I'd

speaker: UC
words: be allowed to, I'd have to be brought home. I'd be down there listening to

speaker: UC
words: all that stuff.

speaker: SAM
words: Well what was that like for her to cook? Did she say?

speaker: SAM
words: hi C: Oh yes, she was apioneer woman! As Isaid in my book, when she got started

speaker: SAM
words: she said "I'm going to be a pioneer woman" and she lived it to the full. Mrs.

speaker: SAM
words: Roberts was a woman who loved life and she lived it. She was a wonderful

speaker: SAM
words: person. And Mr. Roberts was the kind of a fellow that loved life too and he

speaker: SAM
words: lived it up too, but he didn't have her persistence, and he wasn't as stable

speaker: SAM
words: in his likes and dislikes as she was, and he had amore mercurial temperament,

speaker: SAM
words: you know. He was up in the clouds or down in the dumps. She wasn't; she understood

speaker: SAM
words: that you had to take the sunshine with the shadow. And of course she

speaker: SAM
words: never did put that across to Mr. Roberts quite, never got it all the way

speaker: SAM
words: across. They were a delightful pair of people, and very entertaining. And

speaker: SAM
words: in that day and age when you depended on what people said to entertain you,

speaker: SAM
words: they were in great demand always. Al Roberts was always in great demand,

speaker: SAM
words: and when he was a county assessor, he liked to be a deputy assessor because

speaker: SAM
words: he got out on his riding horse, and he had these big saddle bags carrying

speaker: SAM
words: the paraphernalia that went with the deputy assessor's office. And he'd

speaker: SAM
words: carry them along, and he was always very welcome in all the homes: "Come on

speaker: SAM
words: in and stay, Al!" And then when Al got ready to leave: "Well if ever you

speaker: SAM
words: come by my place, if you're ever on American Ridge, find out where Ilive,

speaker: SAM
words: everybody knows, Ilive in the middle of the ridge, and you're perfectly

speaker: SAM
words: welcome to stay all night." And they had lots of lodgers took advantage of

speaker: SAM
words: that, many of them. They never knew when they were going to be alone, they

speaker: SAM
words: had lots of people coming in. And when he died Icouldn't go to the funeral,

speaker: SAM
words: we lived over in Idaho County at the time and Iremember my husband being

speaker: SAM
words: away on a cattle drive...

speaker: SAM
words: Can you expand on what made them entertaining to other people?

speaker: SAM
words: W C: Their personalities, and the way they lived! You find a person that loves

speaker: SAM
words: life and sees life in all its angles, and more than just acommon ordinary

speaker: SAM
words: clodhopper, excuse the term. When they tell things it's much more entertaining.

speaker: SAM
words: And besides, you know there's all the difference in the world between one

speaker: SAM
words: person and another telling atale. Lots of people just (chuckles) see things

speaker: SAM
words: they say the way they are. That's an awful way to be. Then there are people

speaker: SAM
words: who look at things through rose-colored glasses. Well, they're not always the

speaker: SAM
words: nicest things to have around either because they're always too happy maybe,

speaker: SAM
words: and people get alittle bit disgusted with them for being so terribly optimistic

speaker: SAM
words: perhaps. Well, you know, what is that little couplet? "The difference between

speaker: SAM
words: a pessimist and optimist is droll / The optimist the donut sees, the pessimist

speaker: SAM
words: the hole." There's that difference. Some get the meat of it and see the

speaker: SAM
words: whole things.

speaker: SAM
words: As I had to say to aman who came to school to see me about why his boy

speaker: SAM
words: didn't get as good grades in an English class as his daughter did—he said,

speaker: SAM
words: "That was agood article." "Yes," Isaid, and "I know it was, it was avery

speaker: SAM
words: good article, but he tried to take the extraordinary and make the ordinary

speaker: SAM
words: out of it, and that wasn't what he was supposed to do. He was supposed to

speaker: SAM
words: clothe that extraordinary in words that would satisfy his audience and create

speaker: SAM
words: the atmosphere, create an atmosphere of certain things." Isaid, "That was

speaker: SAM
words: what we were trying to do, and we were trying to see which ones got the best.

speaker: SAM
words: So the ones that made it the clearest-there's no way for any teacher in a

speaker: SAM
words: grade school, and especially the way they used to grade, to grade them evenly

speaker: SAM
words: and fairly. Irecognized that factor and always said so. But if we were going

speaker: SAM
words: to have to grade them, aside from saying this is very satisfactory, or giving

speaker: SAM
words: them a few words—they didn't want words, they wanted letters, they wanted a

speaker: SAM
words: real grade.) But Isaid, "Now she isn't that way. If Tommy wants to tell

speaker: SAM
words: about the first day of school with his little brother, he said, 'Well, then

speaker: SAM
words: that year my brother started to school, and he walked along with me, and the

speaker: SAM
words: girls went along with the girls. They went to the same school but they went

speaker: SAM
words: along with the girls.'" But Isaid, "Marilyn didn't say it that way. She

speaker: SAM
words: said, 'The day my little sister started to school, Itook her by the hand,

speaker: SAM
words: and it was abeautiful day. I'll never forget the day. The sun was shining

speaker: SAM
words: so brightly and Italked to her about it. And she said "Well, isn't the sun

speaker: SAM
words: shining because I'm going to school?"' And she said, 'I told her yes, it was

speaker: SAM
words: shining for her.' And she said, 'I felt so proud, and when we passed people

speaker: SAM
words: on the way,' she said, 'I'd wave and she'd wave too. We waved so gaily at

speaker: SAM
words: them. And she'd say to me, "Do they know I'm going to school and this is my

speaker: SAM
words: first day of school?"' And I said, "All the kids sat up and took notice, and

speaker: SAM
words: they could see little Susan doing that. There's a difference." I said, "Now

speaker: SAM
words: do you live by the fact alone, or do you also take in what's on the periphery?

speaker: SAM
words: Well, if you don't, you're missing a lot of life." Well now it's that way

speaker: SAM
words: in this stuff too.

speaker: SAM
words: And Mrs. Roberts really had a sense of that?

speaker: SAM
words: 'w C: Oh yes, and she also had a sense of the time that it was. Yes, she knew

speaker: SAM
words: that. Oh yes, she was great. And she lived out there in that little old

speaker: SAM
words: house. It wasn't quite a log house; they'd built over a log cabin, one of

speaker: SAM
words: the first frame structures. It was never painted, but we objected to her

speaker: SAM
words: ever painting it. But I couldn't go to the funeral. My husband had been on

speaker: SAM
words: a trip with cattle to Spokane, and he came home and he said, "Well, do you

speaker: SAM
words: want to go? I could take you down tonight to the train. You could go over

speaker: SAM
words: in the morning. You'd be late, but then they'd be glad to see ya." And I

speaker: SAM
words: said, "No, but I'll just write to them." So I wrote to Mrs. Roberts and

speaker: SAM
words: oh of course that was a very personal, very private letter. But I did say

speaker: SAM
words: for

speaker: SAM
words: to her that/Mr. Roberts I always will remember Samuel Fosse's poem, "Let Me

speaker: SAM
words: Live in My House by the Side of the Road and Be a Friend to Man." And I

speaker: SAM
words: wrote a stanza of the poem. And she wrote back and said, "Who did you say

speaker: SAM
words: wrote it and how will I get it?" She said, "I want to have it copied on

speaker: SAM
words: Al's tombstone," and it's out there on his tombstone. That's what he thought.

speaker: SAM
words: He didn't care for anything else. And he always went hunting ever year, and

speaker: SAM
words: he always took one fishing trip and he had such a glorious time at it. And

speaker: SAM
words: he came back, now he said, "Now what'11 Frank May get out of life?" I knew—

speaker: SAM
words: but I don't believe I said it then—that he had a pretty large size tin box

speaker: SAM
words: full of mortgages, a mortgage on almost every farm on American Ridge. They

speaker: SAM
words: took 'em out to show 'em to me once. I don't know why somebody was always

speaker: SAM
words: parading his achievements to me. I was a young girl at the time. But anyway

speaker: SAM
words: they worked the only girl they had to death.

speaker: SAM
words: MAVIS LEE UTLEY: Who was this now?

speaker: SAM
words: W C: Frank May. Dora May's father. Poor old Dora didn't get anything out of

speaker: SAM
words: life.

speaker: SAM
words: Al said—

speaker: SAM
words: W C: Well he said, "What's old Frank May going to get out of life? He never had

speaker: SAM
words: a day of fun in his life. He's never had one day of fun." And he said, "Now

speaker: SAM
words: look at me. I don't have a lot of money, but," he said, "my crop turned out

speaker: SAM
words: pretty good. Got a dandy stand of alfalfa up here on the hill, and the

speaker: SAM
words: hogs'll eat well next spring," he said. "And we've got chickens and if nothing

speaker: SAM
words: else," he said, "Fannie keep those chickens going and we'll have butter

speaker: SAM
words: and egg money." He was always full of fun. Fannie, that was Mrs. Roberts,

speaker: SAM
words: her name was Francis Elizabeth.

speaker: SAM
words: How did Frank May come by the mortgages?

speaker: SAM
words: W C: He bought 'em. He had a little money when he came there. He never spent

speaker: SAM
words: a cent on anybody. He never gave away a cent. And I understood that he had

speaker: SAM
words: gambled and got some stuff in the early days by gambling...And then they would

speaker: SAM
words: come to him and ask him for money when they were in a tight spot, and he was

speaker: SAM
words: a very good about letting his neighbors have it. But of course he didn't

speaker: SAM
words: let 'em have free of charge. They paid the usual amount of interest, whatever

speaker: SAM
words: it was. And nobody held it against him, I don't hold it against him, if he

speaker: SAM
words: had the money and he did them a favor of loaning it to them. But I said then

speaker: SAM
words: to Mr. Roberts, "Well you get fun out of different things! He gets fun out

speaker: SAM
words: of getting another mortgage and putting in the box and nearly filling it up

speaker: SAM
words: with his mortgages. Now that gives him deep inner satisfaction. And you get

speaker: SAM
words: deep inner satisfaction when you get away from the grizzly without the grizzly

speaker: SAM
words: getting you, up in the hills. That gives you a deep moral satisfaction.

speaker: SAM
words: It'd give me deep satisfaction too, but I don't krow if it'd have anything

speaker: SAM
words: to do with my well-being except that I was alive yet."

speaker: SAM
words: Did Al ever tell you any of the kinds of things that happened to him out

speaker: SAM
words: on the expeditions in the back country?

speaker: WC
words: Oh he was always telling about something, running into a grizzly at night

speaker: WC
words: or something. He wasn't as good a storyteller as Mrs. Roberts. If she'd

speaker: WC
words: been up there, she would have made it more dramatic. But he had some dramatic

speaker: WC
words: tales and people used to come in to listen to him telling 'em. I didn't think

speaker: WC
words: he dressed them up near enough, you know, really. They were pretty matter

speaker: WC
words: of fact. Or telling about how they went to bed one night tired out or soaking

speaker: WC
words: wet out in the storm, and they'd been going all over one place or another.

speaker: WC
words: And they heard an awful noise, and the horses were squealing, and they knew

speaker: WC
words: something had happened and wondered what in the world was the matter. And

speaker: WC
words: one fella said, "That's bear!" He said, "If they smell bear, they'll make a

speaker: WC
words: noise just like that. That's bear. We've got to go." So up they got, rain

speaker: WC
words: or no rain, and got their rifles loaded, and oh they hated it. They'd cleaned

speaker: WC
words: their rifles at night, as tired as they were. And they went out and there

speaker: WC
words: were three big old grizzlies. And they didn't know, they didn't usually go

speaker: WC
words: in three, never saw that, a pair you could see once in awhile. And they didn't

speaker: WC
words: know they'd gotten into grizzly country, and that's what one of the guys said,

speaker: WC
words: "We got into grizzly country." Well it so happened that they decided the grizzlies

speaker: WC
words: had gotten off their beat and had come down there. But Mr. Roberts assured us

speaker: WC
words: that they got all three of 'em (chuckles). I don't know whether they did or

speaker: WC
words: not. And he had all kinds of trophies, you know, like moose horns and elk

speaker: WC
words: horns and all kinds of little deer horns. Of course he sold a lot of those to

speaker: WC
words: people that came and wanted them. But their house was filled with them, and

speaker: WC
words: Mrs. Roberts got good and tired of having 'em around there sometimes. The

speaker: WC
words: moose horns stood up on the organ, she'd have to clean 'em. "Well you don't

speaker: WC
words: need to clean 'em, do you think the moose cleans 'em all the time?" He said,

speaker: WC
words: "That's all right, let it go." (Chuckles.) And I don't suppose he would've

speaker: WC
words: bothered about it, but she would.

speaker: SAM
words: Well how did Mrs. Roberts tell stories that made her stories so special?

speaker: SAM
words: Because you don't often hear of real good woman storytellers.

speaker: SAM
words: U C: Oh you do too! What kind of a male, what is it...

speaker: SAM
words: Chauvinist?

speaker: WC
words: ...Chauvinist are you? (Mrs. Utlev and Mrs. Carlson laugh.)

speaker: SAM
words: I'm not, I'm not! Ilike to hear it when Ido. But let's put it this way,

speaker: SAM
words: nowdays I think—at least I haven't run into too many yet. You're one of the

speaker: SAM
words: best-

speaker: SAM
words: Hi C: Well then I'm sorry for you, because you haven't heard anybody very good

speaker: SAM
words: then. I'm not especially good at telling stories. You know I don't make a

speaker: SAM
words: habit of telling many stories. Of course children in school—I've been a

speaker: SAM
words: schoolteacher—and they love stories. And they've surprised me sometimes by,

speaker: SAM
words: "Oh please tell this story again. Please tell it." And they're tickled to

speaker: SAM
words: death. Well, I'll tell you one thing they liked with me was I do have a

speaker: SAM
words: sense of humor, and most kids liked my sense of humor. Now and then there's

speaker: SAM
words: one that objects to me; but then as Isay before, Ibelieve in teaching children,

speaker: SAM
words: and when Isee that there's ayoungster that objects to my storytelling, the

speaker: SAM
words: way Itell it, Itry another tact. Iattack him a different way and Itry

speaker: SAM
words: another approach, and pretty soon I find what he likes. And after that I

speaker: SAM
words: have him. I do a certain amount of experimenting.

speaker: SAM
words: Well what about Mrs. Roberts, how did she tell—

speaker: SAM
words: W C: Well she was just her own self and she just told the way it was. She told

speaker: SAM
words: the way it was, and then she had her ringing laugh, and she'd laugh and tell

speaker: SAM
words: how this thing came out, you know. And oh Iknow Iused to ride with them,

speaker: SAM
words: they'd take me along. And he was one of the first ones on American Ridge to

speaker: SAM
words: get a car. And I don't know (chuckles), an old second-hand car—what kind

speaker: SAM
words: was it? Maxwell! A Maxwell car, one of the very first kind of cars that

speaker: SAM
words: was ever put out. And it was hioji. Gee whiz, you had to step up on a step,

speaker: SAM
words: and then you had to step, to get up into the thing. It had no top on it or

speaker: SAM
words: anything, and the steering wheel was imposed upon the car, and here it was

speaker: SAM
words: out in front of him. And he'd get in that and sit up there, you know...He

speaker: SAM
words: was always offering me a ride. My folks didn't like me to ride with him.

speaker: SAM
words: Poppa said that he was gonna scare our team to death one of these times, and

speaker: SAM
words: scare 'em into running away, and there was going to be a mixup, and Al would

speaker: SAM
words: be killed and so would I if I was along. But I still survived. But I'll

speaker: SAM
words: tell ya, sometimes he did have some trouble with that car. Well, usually

speaker: SAM
words: Mrs. Roberts went along; she didn't mind, she liked to ride, she was daring.

speaker: SAM
words: And I loved to go, I'd go anytime I got a chance, and sometimes when I didn't

speaker: SAM
words: have the chance they would let me go, and if Icould get out of asking, why

speaker: SAM
words: I'd get to go. So Iremember that we'd ride in that car and go places. And

speaker: SAM
words: then Mrs. Roberts was along, sometimes, you know, she'd see something in the

speaker: SAM
words: road# "be careful! We're coming to this!" And when we got there

speaker: SAM
words: and it didn't amount to anything, maybe he'd lean over, "Well, Fannie, what

speaker: SAM
words: do you think of that now?" And then he'd ask her, you know, what she thought

speaker: SAM
words: of it. "Did you think that was a black man standing there in the road?"

speaker: SAM
words: And just the way he'd say it to her and her answer, and she'd kind of laugh,

speaker: SAM
words: and of course she was sort of embarrassed and yet the same time amused too—

speaker: SAM
words: she couldn't help being amused by him. And I don't know what made her stories

speaker: SAM
words: different except that she herself was different. It's the individual that

speaker: SAM
words: talks.

speaker: SAM
words: If you compare her to say, what alot of other people were like, not specific

speaker: SAM
words: people, but just what it was that set her off. I'm still trying to understand

speaker: SAM
words: W C: She dared to live. She dared to live. She dared to say what she the

speaker: SAM
words: Men were talking politics and saying a lot of things that she knew was foolish

speaker: SAM
words: ness, and if they would have admitted it, they'd know it. But she would chime

speaker: SAM
words: in with some little remark that actually, if they'd have only known it, cut

speaker: SAM
words: them off at the base. And it would be rather funny to anybody that was looking

speaker: SAM
words: on and listening and knew exactly what had happened. And Mr. Roberts would

speaker: SAM
words: feel piqued. Oh they just had lots of little jokes between them and it'd

speaker: SAM
words: always tickle people. Now my folks lived right across the road from them,

speaker: SAM
words: and they used to come. In those days people went visiting a great deal. You

speaker: SAM
words: never knew on Sunday morning, when you got up you'd better be prepared. You'd

speaker: SAM
words: better have a cake baked and a little baking done. If you didn't you were

speaker: SAM
words: sure going to be surprised because you didn't. And certainly you weren't to

speaker: SAM
words: be caught without light bread. Now you couldn't run down to the store and

speaker: SAM
words: buy a loaf of bread, had no way to run down to the store. It took the team

speaker: SAM
words: a long time to get there, especially ours, which was a team of work horses.

speaker: SAM
words: Mr. Roberts had a pretty good driving team, cayuses that Ithink he'd bought

speaker: SAM
words: from the Indians. Anyhow, he used to have old Buck and Rowdy, and they were

speaker: SAM
words: buckskin horses. You know what that is—kind of a funny brown, tannish

speaker: SAM
words: brown horse. Old Buck and Rowdy. Rowdy lived much longer than Buck, and

speaker: SAM
words: Buck died of colic, and I was supposed to keep out of the way, but I remember

speaker: SAM
words: watching through the cracks of the board fence.

speaker: SAM
words: I watched many things through the cracks of that board fence. And my

speaker: SAM
words: brother who was four and a half years younger than I, of course didn't want

speaker: SAM
words: me ever to go without him. And if Iwould be taking care of him (and they

speaker: SAM
words: always trusted me to take care of him), Isometimes got by_ with things that

speaker: SAM
words: I never would have gotten by with if he hadn't been with me. But he was a

speaker: SAM
words: nuisance. And I would help him up a couple of boards on the fence. Had lots

speaker: SAM
words: of board fences, and they also had arail fence. And we used to climb up on

speaker: SAM
words: louqht.

speaker: SAM
words: that rail fence, watch things. We were perfectly safe on there looking out

speaker: SAM
words: through. But one time we went down to look when they were getting ahunting

speaker: SAM
words: party ready, and we weren't supposed to look. And there we were, looking

speaker: SAM
words: through the boards of the fence so interested that we forgot to hide our

speaker: SAM
words: selves. And so we showed, and enough of my face showed to show who I was.

speaker: SAM
words: And of course as soon as they had that picture ready and showed it around,

speaker: SAM
words: why everybody just laughed, why here was Willa and Orville, (laughs) looking

speaker: SAM
words: through that fence. We were having agood time. We saw the hunting party

speaker: SAM
words: off, but my mother didn't know it until she saw it in the picture. So such

speaker: SAM
words: things as that happened—

speaker: SAM
words: Of course it was just alittle way to Roberts'. Of course here'd come Poppa

speaker: SAM
words: down there yelling for us every now and then, we'd have to show up. But we

speaker: SAM
words: knew enough to stay out of the way. They said we'd get hurt down around

speaker: SAM
words: among those pack horses. You see, people came from all around, Chicago men,

speaker: SAM
words: especially two men, people, one by the name of Ford Robbins and one named

speaker: SAM
words: Dr. Whittaker, who was and M.D. from Chicago. They came out to Moscow, they

speaker: SAM
words: knew somebody by the name of Monroe Hibbs up here at Moscow. Now Ionly

speaker: SAM
words: knew him through seeing him at Mr. Roberts' sometimes. But they would come

speaker: SAM
words: out, and of course they had plenty of money to but a good camping outfit.

speaker: SAM
words: And they would come, getting their camping outfit, and Mr. Roberts was to

speaker: SAM
words: rustle the horses. And he'd get these Indian ponies or some kind of ponies,

speaker: SAM
words: and they'd have eleven and twelve ponies.

speaker: MLU
words: Did you say Mr. Roberts rustled horses?

speaker: UC
words: Well, not in the sense that he stole them! Imean he got out and hustled
